Create a legal brief indicating how the ICC would have jurisdiction, cite the Rome Statute, ICC Jurisdiction and ICL jurisdiction (if applicable). Also include a Section indicating why including CAH is more convenient than also adding WC or Genocide.

Please explain the Northern Ireland protocol to me as if I were a labradoodle. I tuned off after a couple of years of listening to them arguing where "the hard border" is going to be, but the best I reckon there isn't any, anywhere.

We're interested in a construction dispute. The architectural firm we retained to construct our new manufacturing facility provided us with drawings that were insufficient, as they led to errors in the piping of the facility. This was discovered after two and a half years of construction had passed, and incurred us significant re-work and delay costs. What legal recourse do we have against them? We are in Ontario by the way

I'm advising the matriarch of a multigenerational family with significant assets spread across several jurisdictions - including New York, Delaware, and the Cayman Islands - on the restructuring of her family trust. The goal is to balance asset protection, tax efficiency, and intergenerational control without triggering adverse gift or generation-skipping transfer (GST) tax consequences.

The complication is that one of the beneficiaries, her son, recently divorced under New York law, and there's concern that his ex-spouse may assert a marital property claim against his beneficial interest in the trust, even though the trust was created prior to the marriage and includes spendthrift provisions.

Given the current state of New York's equitable distribution jurisprudence, how might we best structure or amend the existing irrevocable trust to minimize the risk of the ex-spouse's claim being recognized by the court - while serving the integrity of the GST exemption and avoiding a deemed transfer for tax purposes?

I represent a global asset management firm operating multiple registered investment advisers and private funds that invest in digital assets and blockchain-based financial products. The client is seeking to expand its tokenized asset platform to allow fractional interests in private credit facilities to be traded among qualified purchasers via a distributed ledger operated by a consortium of financial institutions.

We're chiefly concerned with whether the operation of this platform could implicate registration or custodial obligations under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and the Investment Advisors Act of 1940. Could you provide an analysis of: (1) how the SEC has defined "exchange" and "broker" activity in the context of distributed ledger systems; (2) interpretive issues surrounding digital asset custody and qualified custodians; and (3) potential structuring approaches that could mitigate regulatory risk while maintaining transactional efficiency?

My client is one of three co-defendants in a criminal matter in the Michigan 14th District Court.  We are still at the preliminary exam stage.  She bonded out before I took the case. Conditions of bond were random drug tests and no contact with her daughter or any co-defendants.  S

I heard about the case when I was at the courthouse.   I ran down to find the family of the accused I told them that since she was such a great case, I would do the work on a payment plan and they could stop paying me immediately no matter what they owed if she lost.  They agreed to pay me $200 per month.  

Prior to the first preliminary exam hearing I attended, I noticed that there was no record in the transcript of the complaining witness (a sheriff) being sworn in.  I thought this would be a good reason to stall the case and maybe get it thrown out.  I did an oral motion in front of the court at the next hearing to dismiss the case due to the lack of swearing in.  The judge said swear ins are done at start of a day when the same sheriff is the complaining witness so it's not on the transcription but it does happen.  I said I needed a record or the case should be dismissed..  He dismissed the case and brought the officer in from the sheriff's office next door.  After reopening the case as a new matter, he stated my client would be sent to jail and bail was set at $600.  

I felt so bad about this that I figured I would use the money the family was going to give me toward the client's bail.  Since they hadn't paid me $600 yet, I spend my own money to bail her out, but made a mental note to add that to the client's account.  

Later, y client asked me to pass a letter to her co-defendant's attorney.  I gave it to the co-defendant as the attorney is a jerk.  I also let her borrow my phone during recess.  It turns out she called her daughter and said happy birthday.  As for the letter, the co-defendant told his attorney about the letter. It turns out that the letter was a letter to her co-defendant discussing what she planned to say to the court about the crime.  She asked him to tell the court the same story.  The other attorney told me that his client told him all of this information.  He said he thinks he should tell the judge. Now I'm worried that I will be in trouble just for trying to help my poor client! I don't think any of this was actually unethical behavior as I was doing right by my client.  What ethical issues should I be worried about and what are the possible defenses?

I created a crypto trading firm during the pandemic. In the last few years, it has grown prodigiously, and I have taken in a few other limited partners, but continue to hold the majority voting shares and steer the ship.

With the current state of Washington D.C. fully supportive of crypto as a marketable asset that can even be included in retirement accounts, the enhanced regulations in the country have flattened the playing field for my firm, and I no longer have the same advantages as I did years ago.

I'm thinking about redomiciling my firm outside of the United States to regain at least a tax advantage over what I currently have now as a Delaware LLP operating out of New York City. Can you compare the tax benefits of structuring my LLP in the Bahamas versus Delaware and note the legal liabilities I might incur for doing so domestically?

I represent a multinational client headquartered in the U.S. that's considering acquiring a majority stake in a European renewable energy firm. The transaction would involve a complex web of cross-border subsidiaries, several of which are domiciled in jurisdictions with robust foreign investment screening mechanisms (notably France, Germany, and the Netherlands).

My question consists of two parts: (1) how have recent EU reforms been implemented in practice with respect to non-EU investors acquiring controlling interests in critical infrastructure sectors like energy?; (2) can you summarize any recent enforcement trends or notable decisions by national screening authorities that might inform how this transaction could be perceived from a national security standpoint?

Assume the transaction value exceeds one billion euros and the U.S. parent company has minor defense-sector contracts with the U.S. government.

I'm advising a private equity sponsor on the acquisition of a portfolio of logistics centers spanning New York, Delaware, and Texas, each held through separate single-purpose LLCs ultimately owned by a Delaware parent entity. The transaction will be structured as a merger of the Delaware parent into a newly formed acquisition vehicle, with the real property interests transferring indirectly through the merger rather than by deed.

Can you analyze to what extent each jurisdiction - New York, Delaware, and Texas - treat the indirect transfer of the underlying real property as a taxable event for purposes of real estate transfer tax (or documentary stamp tax, if applicable)? Please provide your answer in paragraph form without the use of bullet points, lists, charts, and the like.

My client is a not-for-profit hospital and is a 501(c)(3) organization. They are negotiating a clinical trial agreement with an industry sponsor. The contract provided by the sponsor states that all inventions discovered during the course of the trial automatically belong to them (the sponsor). They want to hospital to assign all of its rights to IP to the sponsor. As a 501(c)(3), I am not sure that my client can agree to that as it may construed as a private benefit and put their 501(c)(3) status at risk. What are the main legal issues that are implicated? Please provide the specific regulations. What would be the best way to construct contract language to protect the hospital's obligations and allow the sponsor to benefit from any inventions or IP that might result from the trial?

A domestic worker from the Autonomous City of Buenos Aires came to consult us. Se had been working for three years and sent a telegram of resignation to her employer while the worker was on sick leave for three months, which was properly notified. The worker consulted us the day after sending the resignation telegram, and claimed that she was taken to the post office by her employer, with the telegram written by the latter, under threat of reporting her for theft of belongings from her property. The worker claims that she did not want to resign and that her employer intervened after a month o leave to exert pressure and force her to resign, alleging facts that the worker is unaware of.

 What measures should be take to protect the worker's rights? What factors should be taken into account considering the circumstances in which the events occurred, and what regulations and case law would be applicable to substantiate the case? How does DNU 70/23 affects this?

i have made an app that takes a medical history from patients in a structured, repeatable and deterministic way. how can i protect it from competitors like updoc or medmate, who might try to implement the same thing on their platform?

I have been awarded a composite judgment by a Canadian trial court for damages arising from defamation and multiple instances of copyright infringement. The defendant lives in Mississippi. I don't have the budget for protracted litigation in the US courts. Should I seek to enforce both components of the Canadian judgment?

I am a VP in the marketing department at XynoLabo, a mid-sized biopharmaceutical company based in Cambridge, MA. We have several fascinating drugs in development and others currently on the market, but none of which hold a candle to what we will bring to market next year: an MRNA-based treament for RSV!

This is my tenth year with the company. I have seen all my vested stock do well over time and would love to ride out another decade with them until I can retire when I am 60.

Can you let me know how I might be able to structure a 5-year investment plan that capitalizes on the optionality next year and beyond. Include the risk exposures from a legal and financial basis that I might incur from doing so. I have about $500,000 that I'm willing to put to risk here. The company has never let me down in the past.

we have a difficult scenario at work. i am the owner of a healthcare business in australia, and we have both face to face and virtual-only branches to the business. one of the doctors contracted to the virtual-only business has a high-needs child at home, so they work from home and can perform their carer's duty. the contract says he can work as freely as he wants, but it is starting to cause me operational issues and patient losses because some days he can work the entire shift he signs up for, other days he completes half of it and then needs to go because his child needs him. i am aware this is a fine line for discrimination, but what are the options here?

Trying to figure out how best to structure some of my insider stock purchase planning, as I just began to sit on the board of JanBio, a small publicly traded biopharma company, earlier this year. As for my compensation package, they gave me $200K in salary, $800K in restricted stock options, and $500k in stock that is now permissible to be traded based on my having sat in the board seat for more than 180 days.
I have the question about joint-tenancy severance in Ontario. I'm working on a file where two ex-partners co-own a house.. and I need to apply williams v Hensman and Hansen Estate v Hansen properly

An American cartoonist (my client) recently created a successful Youtube cartoon show that's grown to 1 million subscribers. One day the American was visiting Rome, Italy and found multiple carts that were selling t shirts with the cartoon character on them. One of them had a different outfit than the cartoon's typical outfit with crazy sunglasses. A different rendition of the shirt had the character dress as a worker's right advocate related to a popular movement in Italy. Another shirt was a warped version of the character that mocked one of the famous jokes in the show. What can I do for my client in terms of defending his copyright in Italy? If he were to find the same shirts in the US, what would be the analysis under US law out of curiosity?
